title: Electrical Power and Energy
subtitle: P = IV, P = I²R, E = Pt

steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: A 100 W light bulb and a 60 W light bulb are both connected to the mains (230 V). Which has the higher resistance?
options: The 100 W bulb (higher power means higher resistance)
The 60 W bulb (lower power means higher resistance)
Cannot tell without knowing the current
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! At the same voltage, a LOWER power bulb draws LESS current, which means it has a HIGHER resistance. Using P = V²/R, a smaller P with the same V means a larger R. The 60 W bulb has more resistance than the 100 W bulb.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! At the same voltage, lower power means less current flows, which means higher resistance. The 60 W bulb has more resistance — from P = V²/R, a smaller P at the same V requires a larger R.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.2.4 — Electrical power and energy transfer

steps.title: Electrical Power and Energy
steps.bodyHTML: Electrical power is the rate of energy transfer. It is measured in watts (W). There are three key equations: P = I × V (power = current × voltage), P = I² × R (useful when you know current and resistance), and E = P × t (energy = power × time). Energy transferred can also be calculated using E = Q × V (energy = charge × voltage).

relationships: Bigger I → more power (more charge flowing per second)
Bigger V → more power (more energy per charge)
P = I²R links power to current and resistance directly

commonErrors: Forgetting to convert time to seconds when using E = Pt
Using the wrong power equation — choose based on which values you have

steps.title: See it in action
steps.instruction: Drag the sliders to change current and voltage. Watch how power changes with P = IV.

aqa.questionText: A kettle is rated at 2300 W and is connected to the 230 V mains supply. (a) Calculate the current drawn by the kettle. (b) The kettle is used for 3 minutes. Calculate the energy transferred.

equation.examTip: Choose the right power equation based on what values you have: P=IV (given I and V), P=I²R (given I and R), or E=Pt (for energy). Always convert time to seconds.

boardNotes.aqa: P = IV and P = I²R both on AQA formula sheet. E = Pt also given.
boardNotes.edexcel: All three power/energy equations on Edexcel sheet.
boardNotes.ocr-gateway: P = IV on formula sheet. P = I²R may need to be memorised. ⚠️
boardNotes.ocr-21c: P = IV on formula sheet. Check which forms are provided.
boardNotes.wjec: All power equations provided in WJEC exam.
